coredata.dc:title: A smartphone-based ratiometric fluorescent sensing system for on-site detection of pyrethroids by using blue-green dual-emission carbon dots
coredata.dc:creator: Zhu, Xuecheng
Han, Luxuan
Liu, Huilin
Sun, Baoguo
coredata.dc:description: Exploring the high-performance fluorescence strategy for on-site monitoring of pyrethroids is of great significance to the protection of the ecological environment and human health. Aiming at attaining precise onsite analysis, blue-green dual-emission carbon dots (CDs) were used as well-resolved optosensing centers, and an innovative ratiometric fluorescence core-shell nanosphere was constructed using molecular imprinting in this study. The ratiometric fluorescent signal of the CDs was used by the nanospheres to realize on-site quantitative detection of pyrethroids with excellent selectivity. The detection range of this ratiometric sensing platform was 1–150 μg/L and the limit of detection (LOD) was obtained, as low as 0.048 μg/L. Importantly, the core-shell nanospheres exhibited fluorescence changes on exposure to pyrethroids under UV irradiation. A smartphone application was used to output the color channel values of the captured images. The smartphone-based ratiometric sensing system was used for the quantitative detection of pyrethroid with a sensitive LOD of 0.61 μg/L, which could meet the needs of real analytical samples. This sensing platform showed great application potential for the on-site detection of pesticide residues in agriculture food and pollution control in resource-limited environments.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Optosensing
Surface imprinted polymer
Pesticide residues
Fruit
Vegetable
Tea

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of CDs and the core–shell nanospheres
Core-shell nanospheres construction and recognition mechanism
Ratiometric sensing and smartphone scanning dual modes

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic diagram of the prepared core–shell nanospheres for ratiometric sensing and smartphone scanning dual-mode detection of λ-Cyhalothrin (LC).

(A) The main bonding reactions in the preparation of the core–shell nanospheres. (B) UV absorption spectrum of o-CDs (blue) and LC (red), and the optimal emission spectrum of o-CDs (green). (C) Transient fluorescence spectrum of o-CDs. (D) Transient fluorescence spectrum of o-CDs with addition of LC. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

(A) TEM image of core–shell nanospheres with a scale bar of 100 nm. (B) TEM image of core–shell nanospheres with a scale bar of 20 nm (inset: size distribution of core–shell nanospheres). (C) EDS spectrum of core–shell nanospheres. (D) Element mapping images of core–shell nanospheres. (E) TEM image of the control (prepared without LC) with a scale bar of 100 nm. (F) TEM image of the control with a scale bar of 20 nm (inset: size distribution of the control). (F) EDS spectrum of the control.

(A) Fluorescence responses of the core–shell nanospheres to different concentrations of LC (1–150 µg/L). (B) Linear correlation between log[(F550/F440)0/(F550/F440)] of the core–shell nanospheres and the series LC concentration. (C) Fluorescence responses of the control (prepared without LC) to different concentrations of LC (1–150 µg/L). (D) Linear correlation between log[(F550/F440)0/(F550/F440)] of the control and the series LC concentration.

(A) Output fluorescence intensities and fluorescence images with series concentrations of LC, which were recorded and detected using the developed smartphone scanning method. (B) Linear plot of the ratio of blue to yellow (B/Y) as a function of various LC concentration. (C) The B/Y (blue line) and log[(F550/F440)0/(F550/F440)] against the LC concentration (red line). (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
Determination of LC in real samples by the proposed core–shell nanosphere and HPLC.

head.ce:title: A smartphone-based ratiometric fluorescent sensing system for on-site detection of pyrethroids by using blue-green dual-emission carbon dots

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Beijing Advanced Innovation Center for Food Nutrition and Human Health, Beijing Technology and Business University, 11 Fucheng Road, Beijing 100048, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Beijing Advanced Innovation Center for Food Nutrition and Human Health

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Beijing Advanced Innovation Center for Food Nutrition and Human Health, Beijing Technology and Business University, 11 Fucheng Road, Beijing 100048, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Exploring the high-performance fluorescence strategy for on-site monitoring of pyrethroids is of great significance to the protection of the ecological environment and human health. Aiming at attaining precise onsite analysis, blue-green dual-emission carbon dots (CDs) were used as well-resolved optosensing centers, and an innovative ratiometric fluorescence core-shell nanosphere was constructed using molecular imprinting in this study. The ratiometric fluorescent signal of the CDs was used by the nanospheres to realize on-site quantitative detection of pyrethroids with excellent selectivity. The detection range of this ratiometric sensing platform was 1–150 μg/L and the limit of detection (LOD) was obtained, as low as 0.048 μg/L. Importantly, the core-shell nanospheres exhibited fluorescence changes on exposure to pyrethroids under UV irradiation. A smartphone application was used to output the color channel values of the captured images. The smartphone-based ratiometric sensing system was used for the quantitative detection of pyrethroid with a sensitive LOD of 0.61 μg/L, which could meet the needs of real analytical samples. This sensing platform showed great application potential for the on-site detection of pesticide residues in agriculture food and pollution control in resource-limited environments.

ce:section.ce:para: Pesticide residues are a source of pollution in ecosystems, where they can affect food safety and threaten human health. λ-Cyhalothrin (LC) is a typical pyrethroid pesticide that is applied to repel, knockdown, and kill insects, and functions by inhibiting conduction in insect nerve axons (Costa et al., 2020). Accumulation of this pesticide in the human body can damage nerves and lead to a trance-like state or seizures (Zhang et al., 2019). The United States Environmental Protection Agency (U.S. EPA.) has declared that the residual limit of LC is 0.05 mg/g for protection of public health and the environment. Therefore, methods for accurate and efficient analysis of LC residues are important for environmental pollutant monitoring and detection of agricultural food safety.

The main traditional methods currently used to detect pesticide residues are high performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) and liquid chromatography-mass spectrometry (LC-MS). These methods can detect pesticide residues accurately and quantitatively, but they are time-consuming and their pre-processing methods are complicated. These limitations restrict their practical application. Recently, portable and reliable real-time detection technologies have been investigated (Bagheri et al., 2021; Raymundo-Pereira et al., 2021). For example, various fluorescence sensing platforms have been constructed to detect LC using its interaction with certain fluorescent sensors, and these methods will be crucial for the development of on-site real-time detection technologies (Bhamore et al., 2018; Miao et al., 2016). Fluorescence sensing platforms have been developed using carbon dots (CDs) as the sensing signal. As a new kind of photoluminescent carbon nanomaterials, CDs are a powerful substitute for semiconductor quantum dots, whose properties can be adjusted by controlling their size and composition (Sivasankarapillai et al., 2020; Zhu et al., 2021; Yuan et al., 2021). Because of their excellent fluorescence characteristics, zero-dimensional CDs have been used to construct fluorescence sensing platforms (Jia et al., 2019; Junaid et al., 2021; Luo et al., 2021). Most fluorescence detection platforms using CDs have only one fluorescent response signal, which can be easily affected by factors such as instrument deviation or changes in the solvent and produce false positive or false negative results (Biswas et al., 2021; Li et al., 2020). As an improvement to methods with a single fluorescent signal, ratiometric fluorescence methods monitor intensity changes for two fluorescent signals after addition of an analyte in the presence of two dynamic fluorophores. The response ratio of the two fluorescent signals can be used to analyze the target quantitatively (Zhan et al., 2019). Background absorption is effectively avoided and the light source fluctuations are properly eliminated, which can be attributed to excellent self-calibration functions of this method, and the detection sensitivity and limit of detection (LOD) are greatly optimized compared with the single signal sensing method (Chen et al., 2018; Hu et al., 2019; Zhang et al., 2020a). The ratiometric method does not need complex or sophisticated instruments and the LOD obtained can be the same order of magnitude as that for liquid chromatography-tandem mass spectrometry (Lin et al., 2018b; Nardelli et al., 2018).

Recent improvements in smartphone computing power and camera resolution have led to diverse applications of these devices, including in biological and chemical detection (Mukherjee et al., 2020; Sivakumar and Lee, 2021). To meet the requirements for portability and on-site real-time monitoring of modern rapid detection technologies, a variety of smartphone integrated optisensing platforms have been designed using electrochemical current, impedance, fluorescence, and other types of signals (Biswas et al., 2021; Fang et al., 2018; Mei et al., 2016; Yan et al., 2017). Smartphone devices have been used to screen and record color changes created by streptomycin in a fluorescence sensing platform, with the corresponding fluorescence images successfully transformed into RGB data for analysis. These applications have promoted the development of smartphones as signal output devices in sensing and detection (Lin et al., 2018a).

Inspired by the above facts, a simple and effective dual-channel ratiometric fluorescence strategy was developed for the determination of LC based on blue-green dual-emission CDs-functionalized core–shell nanospheres. As shown in Fig. 1 , the blue and green emission CDs were obtained by the solvothermal method as the reference signal and the detection signal, respectively. LC was used as template molecules to create specific three-dimensional imprinted cavities. Ionic liquids (ILs) with wide viscosity range and good thermal stability were used in the system to improve the detection sensitivity and selectivity of the core–shell nanospheres. In the presence of LC, the blue fluorescence at 440 nm was basically unchanged, while the green fluorescence at 550 nm increased significantly, the unique signal response realized the ultra-sensitive detection of LC. In addition, taking into account the requirements of on-site quantification in practical applications, a portable smartphone integrated optical device combined with the proposed core–shell nanosphere was further developed. The significant green to blue fluorescent color evolution enabled instant inspection of LC. The actual application of the developed ratiometric fluorescence sensor and the real-time detection platform based on smartphones was finally confirmed by monitoring the actual food samples with standard addition. This system can realize on-site monitoring of LC residues in samples, and could be used to design sensing platforms for the detection of other small molecule pollutants.

Methanol, ethanol, dichloromethane, acetonitrile, cyclohexane, o-phenylenediamine, m-phenylenediamine, and octadecyl-bonded silica gel were provided from Fuchen Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Tianjin, China). Triton X-100, tetraethyl orthosilicate (TEOS, 98%), 3-aminopro-pyrtriethoxy silane (APTES, 98%) and deltamethrin were obtained from J&K Scientific Co., Ltd. (Beijing, China). 1-hexadecyl-3-methylimidazolium bromide (C16mimBr), LC, fenvalerate, cypermethrin, deltamethrin, and cyfluthrin were all purchased from Macklin Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Ammonia water was from Sinopharm Group Co., Ltd. (Beijing, China). Double-deionized water (A.S. Watson, Beijing, China) was used in all experiments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of CDs and the core–shell nanospheres
ce:section.ce:para: CDs were prepared with both m-phenylenediamine and o-phenylenediamine in a simple one-pot solvothermal process (Jiang et al., 2015). First, 0.4 g of m-phenylenediamine or o-phenylenediamine was uniformly dissolved in 40 mL of ethanol. The resulting solution was transferred to a small high-pressure reactor for reaction at 180 °C for 12 h. The dark liquid was obtained after cooling at room temperature, then this was purified by silica gel column chromatography to remove insoluble impurities and excess reaction reagents. The purified solution was collected and further concentrated by evaporation and then vacuum dried to obtain a solid powder of the CDs based on m-phenylenediamine (m-CDs) or CDs based on o-phenylenediamine (o-CDs). To prepare stock solutions, samples of the m-CDs (5 mg) and o-CDs (15 mg) were accurately weighed and uniformly dissolved in 5 mL of double-deionized water.

First, 300 μL of the m-CDs solution (Section 2.3) and 200 μL of ammonia water were slowly added to 50 mL of a mixture of ethanol and water (v/v, 1:2). Then, 10 mL of a TEOS solution (50 μL of TEOS diluted to 10 mL with ethanol) was added dropwise with stirring state. After stirring in the dark for 5 h, 1 mL of the o-CDs solution (Section 2.3), 50 μL of APTES, and 100 μL of a LC acetonitrile solution (4 mg/mL) were added and the mixing was continued for 30 min. Next, 10 mL of IL solution (1.5 g dissolved in 10 mL of ethanol) and 100 μL of TEOS was added, the stirring was maintained. After leaving the reaction overnight, the mixture was centrifuged (10000 g /10 min). The obtained milky white precipitate was dried under vacuum and then ground to a powder. Then, to remove LC and free small molecule impurities, a mixture of methanol and acetonitrile (v/v, 1:1) was used for Soxhlet extraction, which was repeated several times. The final milky white powder obtained after extraction and elution was a dual emission core–shell nanosphere with specific recognition sites for LC on its surface. To investigate the adsorption efficiency and specificity, another nanosphere polymer was synthesized in exactly the same way but without LC and used as the control group.

A FEI Tecnai G2 F30 microscope (FEI, USA) was used to provide transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images. X-ray powder diffraction (XRD) patterns were obtained with D8 ADVANCE X-ray diffractometer (Bruker, Germany). Fourier transform infrared spectra (FT-IR) were recorded using Vertex 70 spectrometer (Bruker, Germany). Thermo Escalab 250Xi spectrometer (Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA) can give X-ray optoelectronic energy spectrum (XPS) images. Transient fluorescence spectrums were obtained using a FS5 fluorescence spectrometer (Edinburgh, Scotland). Changes in fluorescence were determined using a Synergy H1 microplate reader (Biotek, Winooski, VT, USA).

All fluorescence measurements were performed using a multifunctional enzyme plate analyzer. The light source was a Xenon flash lamp. Measurements were conducted with a photomultiplier voltage of 950 V, scanning step of 10 nm, and slit width of 5 nm. The excitation wavelength was fixed to 380 nm to measure the fluorescence intensity of 550 nm and 440 nm, then the fluorescence intensity ratio (F550/F440) was calculated. A LC standard solution with a set concentration was mixed with a 1 mg/mL suspension of core–shell nanospheres in acetonitrile. To measure the fluorescence signal, 200 μL of the mixture was accurately transferred to a 96-well plate.

Tap water, tea, cucumber and apple were selected as actual agricultural environment and food samples and detected by the developed ratiometric fluorescent sensing platform. Based on the complexity of the sample matrix, all samples had simple pretreatment process. The tap water was directly passed through a 0.45 μm filter, and the filtrate was collected. To obtain the tea samples, 5 g of tea was accurately weighed and immersed in 100 mL of boiling water, soaking time was 30 min, filtered to remove insolubles and impurities, and the supernatant was retained (Zhang et al., 2019). To prepare the cucumber and apple samples, 10 g of each sample was shredded and mixed with 50 mL of methanol. The resulting mixture was ultrasonicated for 10 min, which completely dispersed any compounds on the surfaces of the samples in the solvent, and then the supernatant was collected by filtering (Zhang et al., 2020b). The supernatant from the above four samples was mixed with the core–shell nanosphere solution in acetonitrile, and the fluorescence intensity of the mixture was recorded by a microporous reader after constant oscillation for 15 min. In the subsequent spiked and recovery experiments, four samples were first spiked at three levels 20, 70 and 120 μg/L, each sample was analyzed not less than three times at spiked levels.

First, different concentrations of LC were mixed with core–shell nanospheres, and each reaction solution was pipetted into a 96-well plate. The plate was placed in a portable UV integrated box equipped with a 365-nm light source. Color changes in the fluorescence of the core–shell nanospheres in the plate induced by LC were measured using the smartphone camera. A color recognition program (Spotxel Reader, Version 1.5.5) installed on the smartphone in advance was used to analyze the digital images, the application could identify the fluorescence intensity of the image quickly and converted it into digital information. Purple, yellow, blue, and grayscale values were included.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Core-shell nanospheres construction and recognition mechanism
ce:section.ce:para: Both m-CDs with blue luminescence and o-CDs with green luminescence were obtained by the traditional one-pot solvothermal method, and core–shell nanospheres capable of ratiometric sensing were constructed. First, silica nanoparticles embedded with m-CDs (m-CDs@SiO2) were obtained by the Stober method (Jalili et al., 2020). The SiO2 layer on the outer surface of the m-CDs effectively prevented contact between the m-CDs and the dispersion solvent so the m-CDs could act as a reliable reference background. Next, o-CDs were added to the sensing system as a fluorescent signal. APTES, acting as a functional monomer, first connected the template molecule LC through hydrogen bonding. Then the IL (1-hexadecyl-3-methylimidazolium bromide), which is a cationic surfactant, was attached to the system by electrostatic interaction to enhance the fluorescence of the core–shell nanospheres. TEOS was added and participated with APTES in a co-hydrolysis and condensation reaction to form a layer of imprinted silicon shell on the surface of m-CDs@SiO2. Elution of the template molecules left an imprinted silicon shell containing binding sites complementary to the shape and function of LC that could rebind LC with high specificity (Fig. 2 A.

Fig. S1 shows the fluorescence emission spectra of m-CDs, o-CDs, and the core–shell nanospheres. With an excitation wavelength of 380 nm, the m-CDs had strong fluorescence emission at 440 nm and showed blue luminescence under ultraviolet irradiation. The quantum yield (QY) of the m-CDs obtained using Eq. (1) and quinine sulfate as a reference substance was 11%. Similarly, the o-CDs exhibited optimal fluorescence emission at 550 nm under excitation wavelength at 380 nm and had green luminescence under ultraviolet radiation. The QY calculated for the o-CDs using rhodamine 6G as the reference substance was 17%. These results showed that under excitation at 380 nm there were two consecutive and distinct emission bands at 440 nm and 550 nm for the m-CDs and o-CDs, respectively, which proved that the two prepared CDs were successfully embedded in the core–shell nanospheres.
(1)
Q
sam
=
Q
ref
×
A
ref
A
sam
×
I
sam
I
ref
×
n
sam
2
n
ref
2
where Q sam is the QY of the fluorescent material; Q ref is the QY of the reference substance; A sam and A ref are the absorbance of the fluorescent material and the reference substance, respectively; I sam and I ref represent the integrated areas of the fluorescence intensities of the fluorescent material and the reference substance at the same excitation wavelength, respectively; and n sam and n ref represent the refractive indexes of the fluorescent material solution and the reference substance solution, respectively (Grabolle et al., 2009).

Additionally, the fluorescence emission from m-CDs acted as a good reference background with almost no change when the ratiometric sensing material prepared from core-shell nanospheres was exposed to LC. The fluorescence emission peaks caused by o-CDs showed obvious quenching, and the quenching mechanism was explored. The results indicated that a large portion of the UV absorption spectrum of the o-CDs coincided with that of LC, while the optimum UV absorption peak of LC (210 nm) was distant from the optimum fluorescence emission peak of the o-CDs (Fig. 2B. This result excluded static quenching, internal filter effect and fluorescent resonance energy transfer. Furthermore, the transient fluorescence spectrum of the o-CDs before and after adding LC were obtained and showed that LC as a quencher did not have a significant influence on the fluorescence lifetime of the o-CDs. The transient fluorescence spectrum of the o-CDs without and with LC are shown in Fig. 2C and D. The fluorescence lifetime of the o-CDs was 3.817 ns, and that of the o-CDs with LC was 3.791 ns (Table S1. We speculated that this process conformed to the principle of photo-induced electron transfer (PET), that is, LC could interact with original groups on the surface of o-CDs to form a complex. When the excited electron returned to the ground state, non-radiative emission was initiated by the complex. Specifically, the o-CDs in this study acted as an electron donor, the driving of the energy gap between the lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) of o-CDs and the LUMO of LC lead to a process in which activated o-CDs provide electrons to LC, and fluorescence was quenched (Fig. 2E (Mao et al., 2012).

The morphologies of the m-CDs and o-CDs were studied by high-resolution TEM (HRTEM). The m-CDs were close to spherical (Fig. S2A with an average diameter of 6.9 nm (Inset of fig. S2A, which was calculated using particle size distribution analysis software (Nano Measurer 1.2). The o-CDs were also spherical and uniformly dispersed (Fig. S2B with an average diameter of 7.8 nm (Inset of fig. S2B.

After the two CDs were modified by the Stober method and sol–gel method, TEM showed that the two prepared CDs had been wrapped by molecular imprinting polymer layers, and significant core–shell structure was observed (Fig. 3 A. Because of the use of molecular imprinting technology, the outer surface of the core–shell nanospheres had sites that matched the structure and function of LC, which made the surface of the spheres rough (Fig. 3B. The core–shell nanospheres had a large average particle size of 396 nm (Inset of Fig. 3B. Obviously, the core–shell nanospheres were much larger than the two CDs, which suggested that the shell layer was successfully coated on the surface of the two prepared CDs. Energy dispersive spectroscopy (EDS) of the core–shell nanospheres showed the distribution of C, N, O, and Si in the nanomaterial. The presence of Si provided further confirmation of formation of a SiO2 layer (Fig. 3C. The distributions of C, N, O, and Si were studied by element mapping analysis (Fig. 3D, and were consistent with the results of EDS. The morphology of the control was similar to that of the core–shell nanospheres (Fig. 3E, but the spheres were smoother (Fig. 3F. This result showed that there were no LC imprinting sites. The average particle size was calculated as 382 nm (Inset of Fig. 3F. Additionly, the EDS spectrum of the control group indicated that the distribution of C, N, O, and Si was similar to that in the core–shell nanospheres (Fig. 3G, which indicated that the structure of the control group was highly similar to the core–shell nanospheres excepted that no LC was added as a template molecule.

The obtained m-CDs, o-CDs, and core–shell nanospheres were characterized by XRD. The m-CDs and o-CDs showed obvious diffraction peaks at a 2θ of 24° (Fig. S3A and B, which corresponded to the C (002) crystal plane. The broad diffraction peaks of the m-CDs and o-CDs showed that they may exist in amorphous structures. For the core–shell nanospheres (Fig. S3C, no diffraction peaks related to any type of CDs were detected, which further indicated the amorphous nature of the two CDs.

The FT-IR spectrum of the m-CDs and o-CDs were similar (Fig. S3D, which indicated they contained similar chemical components. A peak at 3039 cm−1 in the spectrum of m-CDs was attributed to the stretching vibration of aliphatic CH. A peak at 1457 cm−1 in o-CDs was assigned to tensile vibration of CN = . A characteristic broad band was observed at 3000–3500 cm−1 in the FT-IR spectrum of the two CDs for hydroxyl groups and amino groups. A strong absorption band at 1629 cm−1 was observed in the spectrums for both CDs and caused by tensile vibration of CO. Peaks observed at 1496 cm−1 for the m-CDs and 1499 cm−1 for the o-CDs arose from the aromatic CC (Vedamalai et al., 2014). These results suggested that decomposition, intermolecular cyclization, and condensation reactions occurred during the synthesis of the two kinds of CDs from m-phenylenediamine and o-phenylenediamine.

Fig. S3E shows the FT-IR spectrum of the IL. A peak at 3082 cm−1 arose from was created by telescopic vibration of the CH of the imidazole ring, and a peak of 2800–3000 cm−1 was attributed to telescopic vibration of CH in the imidazole cycloalkyl substituent. Absorption peaks at 1573 cm−1 and 1472 cm−1 were assigned to vibration of the imidazole skeleton, and a absorption band at 1175 cm−1 arose from telescopic vibration peak of the imidazole ring. The FT-IR spectrum of the core–shell nanospheres is also presented in Fig. S3E. The core–shell nanospheres showed peaks at 2800–3000 cm−1, which proved that the IL was successfully attached to core–shell nanospheres. Asymmetric stretching of Si-O-Si caused a wide and strong absorption peak at 1055 cm−1. A narrow absorption peak at 780 cm−1 was caused by Si-O vibration. A absorption band at 1465 cm−1 was caused by the tensile vibration of C-NH2. These results indicated that the surface of the core–shell nanospheres were successfully coated with silane layers and that –NH2 was abundant on the outer of the nanospheres. These results confirmed that APTES was successfully bonded to the core–shell nanospheres, which meant that detection of LC by hydrogen bonding with the nanospheres would be possible. The FT-IR spectrum of the control was basically the same as that of the core–shell nanospheres, which indicated that the chemical structures of the two groups were similar.

XPS datas of the m-CDs, o-CDs, and core–shell nanospheres further confirmed the FT-IR results. For example, the XPS data indicated that the two types of CDs were constituted of the same elements (C, N, O), the deconvoluted spectra suggested that they contained similar chemical bonds (Fig. S4 and S5. The surface functional groups and elemental analysis of the core–shell nanospheres were also verified by XPS. In the XPS full-spectrum scanning image of the core–shell nanospheres (Fig. S6A, four characteristic peaks were observed at 284.8, 399.7, 532.3, and 103.1 eV for C 1 s, N 1 s, O 1 s and Si 2p respectively. Four characteristic peaks at 284.7, 285.6, 286.2, and 288.1 eV in the spectrum of C 1 s were assigned to CC, CN, CO, and CO groups respectively (Fig. S6B. Characteristic peaks for CN-C and NH groups were observed in the N 1 s spectrum (Fig. S6C. The O 1 s peak-splitting fitting results (Fig. S6D indicated that there were three characteristic peaks at 531.6 eV, 532.9 eV, and 533.0 eV, which correspond to CO, Si-O, and CO groups respectively. In addition to the elements contained in the CDs encapsulated inside the core–shell nanospheres, the XPS spectrums of core–shell nanospheres also confirmed the existence of Si (Fig. S6E. This result indicated that the core–shell nanospheres were successfully synthesized.

We investigated various adsorption solvents for the core–shell nanospheres to help maintain their fluorescence stability and improve the response. The fluorescence intensity ratio (F550/F440) was recorded 2 h and 4 h after uniform dispersal of the core–shell nanospheres in methanol, ethanol, water, acetonitrile, and acetone. Compared with the initial value (0 h), the fluorescence intensity ratio (F550/F440) fluctuated significantly as the dispersion time increased when methanol, ethanol, and water were used for dispersion. After the dispersion time reached 4 h, the ratio dropped by 16.9%, 8.1%, and 14.6% for methanol, ethanol, and water, respectively, and the fluorescence intensity changed significantly. This may be because polar protic solvents are easily accessible in terms of acting as hydrogen donors. The CDs in the core–shell nanospheres combine with hydrogen bonds in the solvent to increase the degree of activation of surface defects on the CDs, and the fluorescence intensity decreases. By comparison, the ability of polar aprotic solvents to provide hydrogen is weak, and the change in fluorescence intensity is small. Therefore, when the core–shell nanospheres were dispersed in acetonitrile and acetone, the fluorescence intensity ratio (F550/F440) did not change over time. The core–shell nanospheres had higher fluorescence intensities in acetonitrile, and this solvent was selected as the final adsorption solvent (Fig. S7A.

Because of the difference between the cation and complex anion of the imidazole IL, it can function as a co-surfactant, solvent, and salt. When participating in formation of the microemulsion system, the IL can induce the formation of materials with a microporous /mesoporous composite structure and different morphologies. Introduction of C16mimBr in construction of the core–shell nanospheres can effectively increase the fluorescence intensity of the nanospheres. We speculated that the original material matrix made energy transfer between ligands more effective. The preparation of core–shell nanospheres was affected by the added volume of the IL. Fig. S7B indicates the influence of the addition of IL on the fluorescence intensity ratio (F550/F440) of the core–shell nanospheres. Because contact between the m-CDs and external solvent was prevented, the optimal fluorescence emission peak of the o-CDs at 550 nm changed with addition of the IL. It When the added volume of IL was 10 mL, the F550/F440 for the prepared core–shell nanospheres was 1.7 times that of the control (IL volume of 0 mL). We also studied the fluorescence quenching effects of LC standard solutions with different concentrations on the core–shell nanospheres. The core–shell nanospheres prepared using different volumes of ILs all showed good fluorescence responses to the series of LC solutions (Fig. S7C. The quenching constants were obtained by a linear fitting process and compared (Fig. S7D. With a low IL volume of 5 mL, the quenching constant was 0.0016. When the volume of the IL was increased to 10 mL, the quenching constant was 0.0040. This difference was mainly attributed to the fact that the IL increased the fluorescence intensity of the core–shell nanospheres, which improved the corresponding fluorescence properties and increased the response sensitivity. However, addition of an excess of IL may inhibit the fluorescence of the core–shell nanospheres because of agglomeration. For the reaction systems with large volumes of IL (15 and 20 mL), the quenching constants were 0.0032 and 0.0029. These results could be explained because the core–shell nanospheres were difficult to dissolved or the recognition sites were blocked during the preparation process due to excessive IL.

The effectiveness of the interaction between the recognition site of the core–shell nanospheres and the LC was evaluated in an adsorption equilibrium study. The adsorption time affected the linear range and accuracy of LC detection using the core–shell nanospheres. After a LC standard solution with a concentration of 150 μg/L was added, the fluorescence intensity ratio (F550/F440) of the core–shell nanospheres was observed over a reaction period of 0–40 min. The F550/F440 of the core–shell nanospheres decreased greatly within 10 min, reached a plateau in about 15 min, and then did not show significant fluctuations after 15 min (Fig. S7E. Therefore, 15 min was considered the optimum adsorption time for the sensing platform. Mass transfer resistance was reduced by the microporous structure of the imprinted layer, and the adsorption time decreased with rebinding of the target material.

The optimum adsorption time was used to explore the ability of the core–shell nanospheres to detect LC through ratiometric fluorescence sensing. When the core–shell nanospheres were exposed to LC standard solutions with different concentrations, the fluorescence intensity of the o-CDs at 550 nm was quenched, and the maximum fluorescence emission peak of the m-CDs at 440 nm was relatively stable (Fig. 4 A. This phenomenon enabled ratiometric detection of LC. These results were calculated by the following formula:
(2)
log
F 550
/
F 440
0
/
F 550
/
F 440
=
K sv
C
+ 1 ,

where (F 550/F 440)0 and (F 550/F 440) represent the fluorescence intensity ratios before and after adding LC, respectively; K sv represents the quenching constant; C represents the series concentration of the added LC standard solution. After calculation, there was a good linear relationship between the series LC concentration and the log[(F550/F440)0/(F550/F440)] (Fig. 4B, the relevant parameters for the regression curve were shown in Tables S2-S4. Simply put, the linear equation was y = 0.00379x − 0.0222, R 2 was 0.988, the linear range was 1–150 μg/L, and the LOD was 0.048 μg/L (signal-to-noise ratio of three). To explore the specificity of the core–shell nanospheres for LC, the fluorescence response was measured for the control (i.e., nanosphere polymer without LC added as a template molecule). The log[(F550/F440)0/(F550/F440)] of the control decreased on addition of LC (Fig. 4C, but a smaller quenching constant (0.000662) and a higher LOD (0.74 μg/L) were obtained (Fig. 4D and Tables S5–S7. The quenching constant of the core–shell nanospheres (0.00379) for LC was 5.73 times that of the control, which showed that the surface imprinting process of the core–shell nanospheres greatly improved the quenching effect and sensitivity. By contrast, the control did not accommodate LC because it was not imprinted. When the recognition site was present in an environment rich in LC, only the fluorescence of o-CDs on the surface of the control was quenched, and the response was not good.

Different concentrations of LC and its structural analogs fenvalerate, cypermethrin, deltamethrin, and cyfluthrin were selected to evaluate the select specificity of the core–shell nanospheres for LC. The fluorescence response of the core–shell nanospheres to LC was obviously different from its response to other pyrethroid pesticides (Fig. S8A. The structural analogs of LC had little effect on the fluorescence intensity ratio of the core–shell nanospheres. The quenching constants were calculated and are presented in Fig. S8B. LC and its structural analogs bind to the same receptor through pairing with the cavity. The adsorption capacity for its structural analogs was much lower than for LC. The quenching constant for LC was 5.7, 5.3, 4.7 and 6.9 times those of fenvalerate, cypermethrin, deltamethrin, and cyfluthrin, respectively (Fig. S8C. In addition, when the nanosphere polymer prepared without LC as a template was used as a control during the preparation process, the fluorescence quenching for LC and its various structural analogs was equivalent and there were no large differences among the quenching constants. These results indicated that the surface of the core–shell nanospheres had recognition sites that LC could strongly bind with, and it had good selectivity for detection of LC.

The anti-interference ability of the core–shell nanospheres was evaluated in competitive adsorption experiments. The LC concentration was held constant and 5, 10, and 15-fold excesses of its structural analogs were added (concentration ratio of LC to structural analog was 1:5, 1:10 and 1:15). After mixing with the core–shell nanospheres, the (F550/F440)0/(F550/F440) were recorded and compared. An increase in the excess of the structural analog had little effect on the fluorescence response (Fig. S8D, and the ratio did not change significantly. This indicated that the core–shell nanospheres formed sites complementary to the size and shape of the LC during the imprinting process, and the LC could bind strongly to these sites and block the binding of other substances (Jalili et al., 2020).

Next, we explored the recognition effect of the core–shell nanospheres for LC when there were interfering substances in the detection system (e.g., common ions or important species in the environment such as amino acids). Aluminum, magnesium, iron(II), iron(III), sodium, calcium, sulfate, tyrosine, potassium, threonine, chloride, nitrate, phenylalanine, leucine, valine, l-theanine solutions, dl-methionine and l-asparagine with a concentration of 1000 nM were prepared and mixed with the LC solution and core–shell nanospheres (concentration ratio of LC to interfering substance was 1:10). The presence of various interfering substances did not greatly affect the ratio of (F550/F440)0/(F550/F440) (Fig. S8E, which further confirmed the excellent selectivity of this sensor material.

The fluorescence stability of the core–shell nanospheres was tested by continuous irradiation with a 365 nm UV lamp. The F550/F440 of the core–shell nanosphere was measured and recorded every 20 min (Fig. S9A. The photoluminescence intensity decreased by less than 0.1% after 120 min of UV irradiation. Additionally, the stability of the core–shell nanospheres over time was evaluated at room temperature with protection from light. The F550/F440 of the core–shell nanospheres fluctuated little over 30 days, and almost consistent detection results were obtained (Fig. S9B. The effect of pH (3–12) on the F550/F440 of the core–shell nanospheres was studied using Britton-Robinson buffer. A strongly acidic or alkaline pH had a slight effect on the F550/F440 (Fig. S9C, and a neutral environment was more suitable for fluorescence emission of the core–shell nanospheres (Miao et al., 2016). In addition, a change in the ionic strength (NaCl solution of 0.1–1.0 mol/L) did not cause a statistically significant change in the F550/F440 of the core–shell nanospheres (Fig. S9D (Wang et al., 2016). Finally, the fluorescence lifetime of the prepared core–shell nanospheres stored in the dark for 4 months at room temperature and 48 h in chiller was tested. It was worth noting that the corresponding fluorescence lifetimes of long-term storage at room temperature and low temperature were 2.84 ns and 2.84 ns, respectively. Compared with the original fluorescence lifetime (2.88 ns), there was no significant change. So long-term storage at room temperature and low temperature conditions did not significantly affect the fluorescence lifetime of the core–shell nanospheres (Fig. S10 and Table S8. These results all showed the core–shell nanospheres had good fluorescence performance and were resistant to decomposition.

Reproducibility represents the ability of the core–shell nanospheres to be used again to bind the template molecules after elution of the adsorbed target molecules. To evaluate this important ability, a 2:1 (v/v) mixture of acetonitrile and methanol was used as the eluent to remove bound LC from the imprinted sites. The same core–shell nanospheres were then used to bind LC again. This process of LC removal and binding was repeated six times. Notably, the detection performance achieved in the sixth experiment was 95% of that in the initial experiment (Fig. S11. The excellent physical and chemical stability and good reproducibility of core–shell nanospheres could be attributed to the highly cross-linked SiO2 matrix on the surface.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Ratiometric sensing and smartphone scanning dual modes
ce:section.ce:para:
The fluorescence intensity of the core–shell nanospheres and self-calibration function of the imprinting site were used to detect LC in complex sample matrices. We selected tap water, tea, cucumber and apple for this study. No LC was detected in these samples using the proposed FL approach and HPLC method. To further verify the practicability and accuracy of the proposed detection system, a spiking recovery study was carried out with spiked concentrations of 20, 70, and 120 μg/L. The related results were calculated and summarized in Table 1 . The recovery rate (Eq. (3)) of the fluorescence detection method based on core–shell nanospheres ranged from 87.93% to 101.4%, which was no statistically significant difference with the HPLC method. These results indicated that the core–shell nanospheres could detect LC in actual samples with high precision. In addition, the sensor performance of the fluorescence method established in this study was evaluated by comparing with various sensor methods for detecting pyrethroids previously developed. As shown in Table S9, the core–shell nanospheres constructed in this study have lower detection limit, excellent reusability, good precision (RSD) and comparable recovery rates in terms of target detection. This further proved that the proposed ratiometric fluorescence sensor provides an effective method to sensitively evaluate pyrethroids in complex real samples.
(3)
Recovery
%
=
Detected
value - Initial
value
/ Added
value × 100 %

The smartphone-based fluorescence sensing platform is executed through three main steps: image acquisition, signal detection, and quantification analysis. Core-shell nanospheres have excellent fluorescence stability. The photographs captured by the smartphones showed that the fluorescence color changed from green to blue as the concentration of LC increased. After detecting and quantifying the color intensity in each well, a normalized signal report was output with color intensity values between 0 and 100 for purple, yellow, blue, and grayscale (Fig. 5 A. There was a good linear relationship (R 2 = 0.977) between the ratio value of blue to yellow (B/Y) and the concentration of LC (Fig. 5B and Tables S10–S12. The LOD determined using a signal-to-noise ratio of three was 0.61 μg/L. The slope of the linear equation between the ratio of B/Y and the LC concentration calculated by the mode of smartphone scanning was very close to the slope of the linear equation between log[(F550/F440)0/(F550/F440)] and the LC concentration measured by the ratiometric sensing platform using the core–shell nanospheres (Fig. 5C. Good recovery rates of 90%–99% were obtained when using tea as an actual sample to carry out a standard recovery experiment (Table S13. These results prove that the smartphone scanning detection mode is feasible for rapid, on-site quantitative detection of LC.

In summary, we have successfully constructed a new type of CDs-functionalized core–shell nanospheres as a ratiometric fluorescence sensor to achieve the selective identification and sensitive detection of trace pyrethroids. Here, IL was added to stabilize the fluorescence signal, due to the modification of the customized imprinting site, the developed core–shell nanospheres showed good selectivity and satisfactory LOD. This low LOD effectively met the requirements of environmental pollution control and food safety testing. The good practical application of the ratiometric fluorescence sensor was finally confirmed by the detection of the spiked actual sample. At the same time, an instrument-free portable fluorescence sensing platform with integrated core–shell nanospheres was successfully constructed for the on-site detection of pyrethroids, which showed good recovery rates and excellent precision in practical applications. This platform is cost-effective, portable, and reliable and could be used to monitor pesticide in the environment and foods on-site and even in ecosystems. Therefore, this work provides new insights in optimizing the strategy of high-quality portable devices and developing an efficient public safety sensing platform. With further optimization, this strategy shows great application potential for nonspecific detection of various classes of pesticides.

Xuecheng Zhu: Methodology, Investigation, Writing – original draft. Luxuan Han: Validation, Investigation, Software, Data curation. Huilin Liu: Resources, Supervision, Writing – review & editing, Funding acquisition. Baoguo Sun: Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 31822040), the National Key R&D Program of China (No. 2018YFC1602300), the Young Top-Notch Talent of High-Level Innovation and Entrepreneurs Support Program (2017000026833ZK28), and the Graduate Innovation Project of School of Food and Health in 2021.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.foodchem.2022.132154.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
